sanskrit_devanagari:
विषया विनिवर्तन्ते निराहारस्य देहिनः ।
रसवर्जं रसोऽप्यस्य परं दृष्ट्वा निवर्तते ॥ ५९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
viṣayā vinivartante nirāhārasya dehinaḥ |
rasa-varjaṁ raso 'py asya paraṁ dṛṣṭvā nivartate || 59 ||

exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.59।। rather than ॥ ५९ ॥)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.059 = MBh_06,024.059). Body text identical: 'viṣayā vinivartante nirāhārasya dehinaḥ | rasavarjaṁ raso'pyasya paraṁ dṛṣṭvā nivartate'. GRETIL writes 'raso'pyasya' as run-together; modern editions print 'raso 'py asya' with the avagraha. Scribal-convention only.

anvaya.meaning: of the abstainer / one who fasts (literally: one without intake)
excepting (in compound: 'with the exception of the taste')

translations.verbatim_quote: Though the embodied soul may be restricted from sense enjoyment, the taste for sense objects remains. But, ceasing such engagements by experiencing a higher taste, he is fixed in consciousness.
Aspirants may restrain the senses from their objects of enjoyment, but the taste for the sense objects remains. However, even this taste ceases for those who realize the Supreme.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Unless one is transcendentally situated, it is not possible to cease from sense enjoyment. The process of restriction from sense enjoyment by rules and regulations is something like restricting a diseased person from certain types of eatables.

Although viṣayāḥ, the objects, recede from an abstinent man, even from a fool who engages in painful austerity and abstains from objects, (still, they do so) rasavarjam, with the exception of the taste, with the exception of the hankering that one has for objects. Api, even that; rasaḥ, taste of the nature of subtle attachment; asya, of this person, of the sannyāsin; nivartate, falls away — i.e. his objective perception becomes seedless — when dṛṣṭvā, after attaining; param, the Absolute, the Reality which is the supreme Goal, Brahman, he continues in life with the realization, 'I verily am That (Brahman).'
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the doctrinal completion of 2.58. Two stages are explicit: (1) the abstainer (nirāhāra) — even a fool who undertakes painful austerity — can cause the sense-objects to recede. But the taste (rasa) for them remains. (2) Only on seeing param (the Absolute, Brahman) does even the taste fall away. Shankara is sharp: 'In the absence of full realisation, there can be no eradication of the hankering.' He addresses the apparent vicious circle (you cannot eliminate hankering without knowledge, and knowledge cannot arise without eliminated hankering) by noting that gross attachment is removed first by discrimination, and full realisation then removes the subtle inclination.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The sense objects are the food of the senses. From the abstinent embodied being, i.e., from one who has withdrawn his senses from objects, these sense-objects, being rejected by him, turn away, but not the relish for them. Relish means hankering. The meaning is that the hankering for the sense-objects does not go away by abstinence alone. But even this hankering will go away, when one sees that the essential nature of the self is superior to the sense-objects and that the realisation of this self gives greater happiness than the enjoyment of sense-objects.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse with a precise mechanism. The sense-objects are 'food of the senses' (a strong metaphor: the senses are eating). Abstinence withdraws the food, the objects turn away, but the hankering remains. The hankering goes only when the operator sees that the self's essential nature is superior to the sense-objects, and that realising the self produces greater happiness than enjoying them. Vishishtadvaita reads param dṛṣṭvā specifically as seeing the superiority of the self / Supreme — the higher pleasure displaces the lower.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: By the body abstaining from food and drink there develops a weakness in the body against experiencing sensual objects. But although the bodily desire is nullified, the inner desire to enjoy the sense objects remains. Thus the rasa or taste for enjoyment is not terminated. Lord Krishna states that this taste can only be terminated by spiritual intelligence and by the attainment of stitha-prajña transcendent meditation.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Madhva separates body-level desire from inner desire. Bodily abstinence (fasting, austerity) weakens body-level desire but leaves the inner taste intact. The inner taste terminates only by spiritual intelligence and the attainment of sthita-prajña meditation. The Dvaita reading emphasises that the verse is naming a real two-stage doctrine, not collapsing them.

positions.rendering: by experiencing a higher taste (param glossed as 'higher taste' rather than 'the Supreme')
for those who realize the Supreme
after attaining param, the Absolute, the Reality which is the supreme Goal, Brahman
when one sees that the essential nature of the self is superior to the sense-objects (param read as the higher reality of the self)
by spiritual intelligence and the attainment of sthita-prajña transcendent meditation

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Sanskrit param dṛṣṭvā is dense — 'on seeing the Supreme'. Each tradition reads param according to its frame: Shankara reads it as Brahman / the Absolute (Advaita); Ramanuja reads it as the superior reality of the self in relation to the Supreme (Vishishtadvaita); Madhva reads it as the attained state of sthita-prajña (Dvaita). Prabhupada introduces the gloss 'higher taste' which is creative — making the doctrinal point sharper (a higher taste displaces the lower) but moving from the literal 'on seeing the Supreme' to a comparative-tastes frame. The verse text itself supports the literal: param = the Supreme; dṛṣṭvā = on seeing. The engineering layer should preserve the structural point: the taste departs only on the arrival of something higher; abstention alone does not eliminate it.

positions.rendering: the taste for sense objects remains. But, ceasing such engagements by experiencing a higher taste…
but the taste for the sense objects remains. However, even this taste ceases…
objects recede with the exception of the taste; even that taste of the nature of subtle attachment falls away…
the relish (rasa = hankering) does not go away by abstinence alone; but even this hankering will go away…

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The verse names a precise sequence: rasa-varjam (excepting the taste) is the residue of mere abstinence; raso 'py asya … nivartate (even that taste departs) is the consequence of seeing the Supreme. All four sources preserve the two-stage structure. The doctrinal point is sharp: the verse says explicitly that the taste does not depart with abstinence — it requires realisation. The engineering layer must preserve this: abstention is incomplete; only the displacement by something higher is complete.

literal_meaning: Sense-objects withdraw from the abstainer, the embodied one, with the exception of the taste; even the taste of his departs upon seeing the Supreme.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse names the doctrinal completion of 2.58. Voluntary sense-withdrawal (the tortoise) is real but partial: the sense-objects recede (viṣayāḥ vinivartante) when the abstainer (nirāhāra) refuses them, but the taste (rasa) for them remains. Even painful austerity does not eliminate the taste. The taste only departs when the operator sees param — the Supreme. Each tradition reads param differently: Shankara as Brahman / the Absolute; Ramanuja as the superior reality of the self in relation to the Supreme; Madhva as the realised state of sthita-prajña. All three agree on the structural point that splits the verse: abstention alone is incomplete; only realisation completes it. The engineering doctrine is: removing the object does not remove the craving; the craving departs only when displaced by something higher.
